This test fails because dct['cb'] is assigned a _LuaFunction value when dct is a Python object accessed from Lua. This _LuaFunction has _state set to coroutine's state, and it doesn't work when a later code tries to call it.

It works if we pass `lua.table_from({})` instead of a regular Python dict because in this case both table wrapper and _LuaFunction object are created in Python code, and so they get main state as their _state.

Something similar happens when `lua.eval("coroutine.resume")(coro)` is used instead of `next(coro)` / `coro.send(...)` - all objects get 'main' state as their `_state`.

`Context()` already has a `version` attribute, making it impossible to reach the `version()` method. So using `version()` will always throw an exception, because it will resolve `version` first as the string attribute, and try to apply `__call__` on it, which will produce a `TypeError`:
```
>>> from pysparkling import Context
>>> Context().version
'0.3.16'
>>> Context().version()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: 'str' object is not callable
``` 
Since the method is unreachable, better remove it.

It doesn't need to be baked into deisctl! Since it's a glorified fleetctl wrapper we can use our own tooling for this!

For example, using the [ping example service from fleet](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/coreos/fleet/master/examples/ping.service):

```
><> cd $DEISCTL_UNITS
><> wget [ping.service]
><> mv ping.service deis-ping.service
><> deisctl install ping
deis-ping.service: loaded                                 
><> deisctl start ping
deis-ping.service: active/running                                 
><> deisctl journal ping
-- Logs begin at Wed 2015-07-01 09:14:37 . --
Jul 01 16:49:27 deis-03 systemd[1]: Started PING.
Jul 01 16:49:27 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
Jul 01 16:49:27 deis-03 systemd[1]: Starting PING...
Jul 01 16:49:28 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
Jul 01 16:49:29 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
Jul 01 16:49:30 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
Jul 01 16:49:31 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
Jul 01 16:49:32 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
Jul 01 16:49:33 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
```

Another solution would be to change the last few calls to fleetctl to the following:

```
$ DEISCTL_UNITS=/tmp deisctl install store-volume
$ DEISCTL_UNITS=/tmp deisctl start store-volume
$ rm /tmp/deis-store-volume.service
```

I agree with @Blystad that using existing tooling would make for a happier installation experience, but the controller fix would also be a better solution so we don't have to hack this in. This is an officially supported workflow so we should make this as smooth as possible for users, in my opinion. :)

OTOH, I don't personally find it very confusing to say that there is a set of flags set at the global level as well as a each unit having a set of flags that are set for it, and the decorators will match either.  I thought that the idea of units having flags on them made sense with the `self.all_units.with_flag(foo)` filter.

If we are going for the other perspective, that flags have a list of units in them, then it seems like it would make more sense for flags to become first class instances, so that you would say something like:

```python
foo = flag('foo')
foo.set()
foo.clear()  # or remove()
foo.add_unit(unit)  # implicitly sets it if not already
foo.remove_unit(unit)  # implicitly clears it if that was the last unit
for unit in foo.units:
    # but how do we only get the units for a specific application?
    pass
```

I feel like this more fundamentally changes the nature of flags, so I don't really like it.  So, my preference is to use the perspective that units can have flags and all flags are visible to the decorators, whether global or on a unit.

A couple problems here:

1. The docstring indicates "dictionary-style". I think we're talking about "list-style" behavior here. Along those same lines, I'd prefer `idx` rather than `key` to impress on the reader we expect a non-negative integer here.

2. `tabs` is only the parent element, it does not have sequence behaviors (despite the naming :). So the `w:tab` element in question here comes from `tabs.tab_lst[key]`, not `tabs[key]`. I'd put that access on a separate line just to keep things easy reading. Also, it produces a marginally more useful error message when someone chooses an index out of range.

3. There are no cases here where indexed access should return `None`. It should return a tab stop or raise an exception. In an empty TabStops object for example, even tab_stops[0] is invalid. The exception should be `IndexError`.

The reason I saw this as problematic wasn't due to the convolution factor. Rather, it has to do with the inclination of the exponential disk. See the maths here for the basis for the calculation here: https://www.dropbox.com/s/z6h14bgd199czsi/Inclined_Exponential.pdf?dl=0 - in particular, look at equation (3), which is before any coordinate transformation. The issue comes from the y^2/cos^2(i) term in the exponential on the right side. In the case where i=pi/2, the assumption that we can integrate across exponential disks breaks down, so instead we have to integrate through a disk, and this doesn't have a closed-form solution that I know of.

The possible problem in Fourier space comes from the fact that as i approaches pi/2, ky*R*cos(i) approaches zero, but the limits for ky approach infinity, resulting in a 0/0 type situation. However, I think the convolution saves us here. Since we multiply by ky/sinh(ky), the k value goes to zero at a non-infinite value. This goes back to your other comment that my calculation of the maximum k was too conservative, since I didn't account for this. So, I think once that issue is resolved, the edge-on case won't be an issue either (it will just take a bit of rewriting the code). I'll have a look at this tomorrow.
